<findings>

[observation] [info] High cohesion (0.8) — files are tightly interconnected
[observation] [info] Bidirectional imports between hench-guard and hench-agent introduce a coupling risk for a policy enforcement layer; review whether the 2 imports from hench into guard can be inverted or replaced with injected dependencies to make guard fully independent of agent internals.
[observation] [info] The guard module's 0.8 cohesion and focused 5-file production surface reflect a well-scoped safety boundary — this is a positive architectural pattern worth preserving as hench grows.
[observation] [info] shell.test.ts is co-located in the guard test directory despite testing a tool in the main hench-agent zone — consider moving it to hench's own unit test tree to keep guard tests scoped to guard logic.
[relationship] [warning] hench-guard ↔ hench-agent is the only bidirectional cycle outside the web package — it is also the only cycle where the smaller zone (10 files) imports into the larger (141 files) rather than the reverse, confirming this is a policy-layer inversion rather than organic coupling growth.
[anti-pattern] [warning] hench-guard imports agent internals (4 cross-zone imports) to enforce policy, making guard correctness dependent on agent implementation details — if agent internals are refactored the guard silently loses enforcement capability; a stable injected policy interface would break this implementation-level dependency.

</findings>

<insights>

- High cohesion (0.8) — files are tightly interconnected
- Cohesion 0.8 with only 10 files (5 prod, 5 test) indicates tight internal relationships — the guard module has a focused, single-responsibility design
- The bidirectional import with hench-agent (guard→hench 4 imports, hench→guard 2 imports) should be narrowed: ideally guard is a pure policy layer that hench calls, not one that also imports from the agent core
- Test coverage mirrors the production surface file-for-file, which is a strong quality signal for a security-critical module
- The guard module's 0.8 cohesion and focused 5-file production surface reflect a well-scoped safety boundary — this is a positive architectural pattern worth preserving as hench grows.
- Bidirectional imports between hench-guard and hench-agent introduce a coupling risk for a policy enforcement layer; review whether the 2 imports from hench into guard can be inverted or replaced with injected dependencies to make guard fully independent of agent internals.
- shell.test.ts is co-located in the guard test directory despite testing a tool in the main hench-agent zone — consider moving it to hench's own unit test tree to keep guard tests scoped to guard logic.
- hench-guard ↔ hench-agent is the only bidirectional cycle outside the web package — it is also the only cycle where the smaller zone (10 files) imports into the larger (141 files) rather than the reverse, confirming this is a policy-layer inversion rather than organic coupling growth.
- hench-guard is the only zone where a smaller zone imports agent internals to enforce policy against the larger zone — in every other inward-coupling pair direction is hub-receives-from-leaf; here guard policy enforcement capability is contingent on agent implementation details, creating a hidden coupling where agent refactoring can silently break guard correctness without type errors.
- hench-guard imports agent internals (4 cross-zone imports) to enforce policy, making guard correctness dependent on agent implementation details — if agent internals are refactored the guard silently loses enforcement capability; a stable injected policy interface would break this implementation-level dependency.
- hench-guard (10 files) has 5 production files and 5 test files with one-to-one test coverage. However, the investigation confirms that packages/hench/tests/unit/guard/ contains only guard-specific test files and does NOT contain shell.test.ts or any tests for non-guard modules. The zone's test coverage is correctly scoped to its production surface, making it one of the most precisely test-covered zones in the monorepo.
- [call graph] 114 internal calls, 53 outgoing, 1 incoming (cohesion: 0.68, coupling: 0.32)

</insights>
